coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of sulfur-doped graphene quantum dots as fluorescent sensing probes for Ag+ ions detection
coredata.dc:creator: Bian, Shiyue
Shen, Chao
Qian, Yuting
Liu, Jiyang
Xi, Fengna
Dong, Xiaoping
coredata.dc:description: Sulfur-doped graphene quantum dots (S-GQDs) with bright blue emission have been prepared by a facile one-pot hydrothermal treatment. A specific compound, 1,3,6-trinitropyrene, which has a mother nucleus structure similar with graphene, was chosen as the carbon source and 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) was employed for S-doping and carboxyl groups modification. The synthesized S-GQDs were characterized by atomic force microscopy (AFM), transmission electron microscope (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and fluoscence (FL) spectrum. Results indicated that S-GQDs possessed single layer graphene structure with mean size of about 2.5nm and presented an excitation-independent photoluminescence behavior with maximum excitation/emission wavelength at 360/450nm, respectively. The sulfur-doping of GODs drastically improved their electronic and chemical properties, which afforded the S-GQDs a sensitive response to Ag+ ions. Furthermore, the S-GQDs were successfully explored as a sensing probe for Ag+ detection with high sensitivity and selectivity. A wide linear range of 0.1-130.0μM with a low detection limit of 30nM was obtained. The facile preparation method and the high performace of the as-prepared S-GQDs present promising potential for their applications in sensing, biological imaging and catalysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sulfur-doped
Graphene quantum dots
Ag+
Fluorescent probe
Selective detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescent detection of Ag+ using S-GQDs
Excitation-independent fluorescence properties of the S-GQDs
Selective fluorescence response of S-GQDs towards Ag+
Detection of Ag+ using S-GQDs as fluorescent probe

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21305127 ), the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China ( Y15B050022 , Y17B050019 ), the Science Foundation of Zhejiang Sci-Tech University ( 13062173-Y ) and 521 talent project of ZSTU. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) FL excitation and emission spectra of the S-GQDs aqueous solution. FL excitation spectrum was obtained at emission wavelength of 450nm. FL emission spectra was obtained with excitation wavelength progressively being increased from 310 to 390nm (wavelength interval between adjacent lines was 10nm). The insets are the photographs of the S-GQD aqueous solution under visible light (left) and a UV light of 365nm (right). (B) The effect of pH value for the FL intensity of the S-GQDs aqueous solutions.
(A) TEM and (B) HRTEM images of the as-prepared S-GQDs, the inset is the corresponding size distribution of the S-GQDs.
(A) Full-scan XPS spectra, (B) High-resolution C 1s, (C) High-resolution O 1s and (D) High-resolution S 2p spectra of the S-GQDs.

(A) the photographs of S-GQDs upon addition of different metal ions in aqueous solution under UV light of 365nm. (B) The F/F0 of the S-GQDs solutions in the presence of various metal ions (135.0μM). (C) The plot of the values of (F0 −F)/F0 at different pH values with addition of 135.0μM Ag+. (D)Time-dependent fluorescence response of S-GQDs upon the addition of 110.0μM Ag+.

(A) FL spectra of S-GQDs upon addition of different concentration of Ag+ (from top to bottom: 0, 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.8, 1.0, 5.0, 10.0, 20.0, 30.0, 40.0, 50.0, 60.0, 70.0, 80.0, 90.0, 100.0, 110.0, 120.0, 130.0μM, respectively). (B) Linear range of the plot of (F0 −F)/F0 against the concentration of Ag+ (0.1–130.0μM).

Schematic illustration for the preparation of blue S-GQDs and turn-off detection of Ag+. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Comparison of different fluorescent probes for Ag+ detection.
Determination of Ag+ in environmental water samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Sample 1 was made by concentrating the water of Jinsha Lake for 50 times. Sample 2 and 3 were obtained by adding Ag+ into sample 1.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of sulfur-doped graphene quantum dots as fluorescent sensing probes for Ag+ ions detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzhou, 310018, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sulfur-doped graphene quantum dots (S-GQDs) with bright blue emission have been prepared by a facile one-pot hydrothermal treatment. A specific compound, 1,3,6-trinitropyrene, which has a mother nucleus structure similar with graphene, was chosen as the carbon source and 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) was employed for S-doping and carboxyl groups modification. The synthesized S-GQDs were characterized by atomic force microscopy (AFM), transmission electron microscope (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and fluoscence (FL) spectrum. Results indicated that S-GQDs possessed single layer graphene structure with mean size of about 2.5nm and presented an excitation-independent photoluminescence behavior with maximum excitation/emission wavelength at 360/450nm, respectively. The sulfur-doping of GODs drastically improved their electronic and chemical properties, which afforded the S-GQDs a sensitive response to Ag+ ions. Furthermore, the S-GQDs were successfully explored as a sensing probe for Ag+ detection with high sensitivity and selectivity. A wide linear range of 0.1-130.0μM with a low detection limit of 30nM was obtained. The facile preparation method and the high performace of the as-prepared S-GQDs present promising potential for their applications in sensing, biological imaging and catalysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, photosensitive nanomaterials have been attractive in bioimaging[1,2] , optoelectronicdevice [3], catalysis [4], matrix for deposition of surface-clean metal nanoparticles [5,6], sensing detection [7–9], and etc. As novel type of zero-dimensional (0D) luminescent nanomaterials and new member of the graphene family, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have ignited tremendous research interests. Owing to the pronounced quantum confinement and edge effects, carbon-based GQDs present extraordinary optical and electrical characteristics which are not obtainable in other kinds of QDs. For example, compared with traditional semiconductor QDs (e.g. CdS, CdSe, CdTe) that are expensive and contain cytotoxic heavy metal constituents, GQDs show many advantages including low toxicity, excellent solubility, stable luminescence, high biocompatibility and tunable band gap [10–12]. Therefore, the development of facile method for easy and controllable synthesis of functional GQDs is now at the center of research efforts. Until now, two major methods inculding top-down and bottom-up strategies have been developed to prepare GQDs. The top-down methods mainly focus on the cutting of graphene sheets into GQDs using concentrated acid oxidation [13], UV-Fenton oxidation [14], electrochemical oxidation [15], microwave auxiliary oxidation [16], etc. However, the top-down method might be limited by low product yield and harsh conditions because of the large usage of strong acid or oxidant. On the contrary, the bottom-up approaches apply small organic precursor molecules to construct GQDs structure through catalytic or thermal treatment processes [11,17]. Environmentally friendly production of GQDs with large scale, homogeneous size and morphology can be achieved.

As recently demonstrated, heteroatom doping (oxygen, boron, nitrogen, phosphor, sulfur, etc.) can change various properties of GQDs including electronic structure, band gap, chemical reactivity, and optical properties [18,19]. In contrast to B, N or P doping, doping of sulfur (S) into the framework of GQDs might be relatively difficult because the formation of CS bond is hard to achieve. The reasons lie in two aspects. On the one hand, S atom is larger than C atom and the CS bond length (1.78Å) is 25% longer than that of the CC bond. On the other hand, the difference of electronegativity between S (2.58) and C (2.55) is very close and negligible polarization (or charge transfer) exists in the CS bond [19]. Consequently, only few studies covered sulfur-doped carbon dots (S-CDs) or GQDs (S-GQDs) had been reported until now. In term of S-CDs, Xu et al. synthesized S-CDs using hydrothermal method starting from sodium citrate and sodium thiosulfate, that was used as probes toward Fe3+ detection [20]. Hu et al. prepared S-CDs by treating waste frying oil with concentrated sulfuric acid, that was suitable for cell imaging [21]. Kwon et al. perpared S-CDs with a strong long-wavelength absorption band using bis(2-ethylhexyl) sulfosuccinate sodium salt, glucose and thioglycolic acid [22]. Chandra et al. prepared S-CDs from thiomalic acid and concentrated sulfuric acid. After adjoin with gold nanoparticles and DNA, potential applications in bioanalysis were evaluated [23]. For S-doped GQDs, Li et al. prepared S-GQDs by electrolysis of graphite in sodium p-toluenesulfonate medium, that was also used as probes for Fe3+ detection [24]. Li et al. prepared S-GQDs by a hydrothermal method using fructose and sulphuric acid as source materials [25]. Though S-GQDs have now rarely been reported, the S-doping in GQDs might greatly extend the arsenal of GQDs and their potential applications. Furthermore, studies on S-doped graphene have proven that the mismatch of the outermost orbitals of S and C will induce a non-uniform spin density distribution, which might consequently endow S-GQDs with surface reactivity or catalytic properties [19]. Therefore, the development of smart bottom-up methodology for preparing novel S-GQDs with high crystal quality is highly desired. Moreover, the investigation on the properties and potential applications of S-GQDs is also necessary.

In this paper, one-pot synthesis of S-GQDs was developed via a simple hydrothermal method. A specific compound, 1,3,6-trinitropyrene, that has a mother nucleus structure similar to that of graphene and three strong electrophilic sites, was chosen as the carbon source. 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) with SH and COOH was employed as the sulfur source for both S-doping and carboxyl groups modification. The as-prepared S-GQDs exhibited blue emission, single layer graphene structure, uniform size, and excitation-independent photoluminescence behavior. As known, Ag+ has been widely applied in industry of electronics, photography, mirrors, and pharmacy [26–28]. However, long term exposure of Ag+ in water might be toxic to animals and human beings. N-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) and amine-terminated GQDs have been reported as FL probe for the detection of Ag+ ion. For instance, Tabaraki et al. prepared N-doped GQDs from glucose and ammonia, that exhibited as sensing probes for Ag+ ions determination with linear range of 0.2–40μM and the limit of detection of 168nM, respectively [29]. Suryawanshi et al. firstly synthesized GQDs by treating bio-waste (dead leaves) with acid mixture of concentrated H2SO4 and HNO3 (3:1 ratio). Afterwards, GQDs was further modified to produce amine-terminated GQDs by ammonia solution using hydrothermal treatment. Those amine-terminated GQDs were applied for detection of Ag+ ion with detection limit of 0.033gL−1 to 0.1gL−1 [30]. Owing to the drastical improvement of the surface chemical reactivities, the application of our S-GQDs for sensitive detection of Ag+ ions were described and discussed in detail.

Pyrene, 3-mercaptopropionic acid, NaOH, NaH2PO4, Na2HPO4 and 4-(2-hydroxyethyl)-1-piperazineethanesulfonic acid (HEPES) were obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (China). Aqueous solutions of K+, Na+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Fe3+, Al3+ were prepared from their chloride salts. Aqueous solutions of Ag+, Cd2+, Pb2+ were prepared from their nitrate salts. All chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received. All aqueous solutions were prepared with ultrapure water (18.2MΩcm, Milli-Q, Millipore).

UV-vis absorption spectum was obtained on a UV–vis 2650 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Corporation, Japan). Fluorescence spectrum was recorded on an RF-5301PC spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu Corporation, Japan). Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) photograph was taken on a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope at operating voltage of 200kV (JEOL Ltd., Japan) to investigate the microstructures and size distribution. Height profiles of S-GQDs were acquired by atomic force microscopy (AFM) images (MFP-3D AFM microscope, Asylum research). Elemental analysis of the S-GQDs was performed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) with a PHI5300 electron spectrometer using 250W, 14KV, Mg Kα radiation (PE Ltd., USA). Inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) was used to give the standard concentration of Ag+ ions (NexloN 300D, PE Ltd., USA).

Using pyrene as the initial substrate, 1,3,6-trinitropyrene was synthesized according to the literature [31]. Then S-GQDs were prepared by one-pot hydrothermal process using 1,3,6-trinitropyrene as the carbon source and 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) as the sulfur source. Briefly, 0.1M MPA and 20mg/mL 1,3,6-trinitropyrene were firstly mixed by ultrasonic for 1h. Then the resulting suspension was placed into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave for hydrothermal treatment at 200°C for 10h. After cooling down to room temperature, the cloudy solution containing water-soluble GQDs was filtered through a 0.22mm microporous membrane to remove insoluble carbon product. Then, un-reacted small molecules were removed by dialyzing in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight of 1000Da) against ultrapure water for 24h. The obtained sample was further dialyzed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 3500Da) against ultrapure water for 24h to remove impurities with large size. After dialysis, a pale-yellow aqueous suspension of the S-GQDs was obtained and freeze dried.

QY was calculated according to the following equation:
Y μ
=
Y S
×
F u
A S
F S
A μ
where Yμ and Ys are the quantum yield of the S-QGDs and the standard substance, Fu and Fs respectively stand for the integral fluorescence intensity of the test S-QGDs and the diluted standard solution and Aμ and As are the maximum absorbance value of S-QGDs and the diluted standard solution, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent detection of Ag+ using S-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of Ag+ was carried out in HEPES buffer solution (2.0mM, pH 7.0)[32]. In a typical run, S-GQDs solution (0.01mg/mL) was dispersed in the buffer and then mixed with different amount of Ag+. After the resulting solution was shaken well and incubated for 10min at room temperature, the fluorescence emission spectra (excited at 360nm) were recorded. The detection measurements were performed in triplicate. The relative fluorescence intensity (F0 −F)/F0 versus Ag+ concentration were used for calibration. Here, F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of S-GQDs in the absence and presence of different concentration of Ag+, respectively.

Analysis of real sample using the as-prepared S-GQDs was also performed using environmental sample matrix. Water of Jinsha Lake (Hangzhou, China) was collected and concentrated 50 times (Sample 1). Sample 2 and 3 were obtained by adding Ag+ into sample 1. The detection results of Ag+ ions in the three samples using the as-prepared S-GQDs and ICP-MS were compared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Excitation-independent fluorescence properties of the S-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of the S-GQDs were investigated by UV–vis spectroscopy. It is clear that the S-GQDs showed a strong absorption peak at ∼400nm that was due to the n–π* transition usually observed in doped graphene systems (Fig. S1). The S-GQDs aqueous solution showed pale-yellow under visible light and emitted strong blue fluorescence under 365nm UV lamp irradiation (inset of Fig. 1 A). As the excitation wavelength changed from 310nm to 390nm with wavelength interval of 10nm, the FL intensity increased when the excitation wavelength increased from 310nm to 360nm and then decreased at excitation wavelength of 375nm and 390nm. The maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of the S-GQDs were revealed at 360nm and 450nm, respectively (Fig. 1A). Furthermore, nearly excitation-independent fluorescence emission curves were observed (Fig. 1A) because the FL emission center was unchanged when excitation wavelength altered. These excitation-independent fluorescence properties indicated that the S-GQDs possessed single-emission fluorescence center and high crystallinity[31].

The effect of pH on the FL intensity of the S-GQDs was studied. A wide pH range from 2 to 10 was used (Fig. 1B). When pH was 2.0 or 3.0, FL intensity was low at such strong acidic conditions, whereas, it increased dramatically with the increase of pH value. When pH was above 5, fluorescent intensity was slightly changed. The phenomenon might be ascribed to the ionization of carboxyl group. It is well known that electronic structure and charge density might affect the opitical characteristics of GQDs. In our one-pot hydrothermal synthesis of S-GQDs, 3-mercaptopropionic acid with −SH and −COOH was chosen as the sulfur source for both S-doping and carboxyl groups modification. When the carboxyl group was uncompletely ionized under strong acid condition, the fluorescence intensity was lower. When pH was 7.0, the QY was 9.2%.

XPS measurements were applied to analyze elemental composition of the S-GQDs. The XPS survey spectrum clearly suggested the presence of C, O, and S with atomic percentages of 57.4%, 39.1% and 3.5% in the S-GQDs (Fig. 4 A). The corresponding C 1s, O 1s and S 2p peaks centered at 264eV, 553eV and 154eV, respectively. The C 1s spectrum of S-GQDs (Fig. 4B) showed the presence of three peaks, which were assigned to CC at 284.2eV, CS at 285.6eV and CO or CO at 287.2eV, respectively[33]. The O 1s spectrum of S-GQDs (Fig. 3C) revealed that there were two peaks at 533.2eV and 531.4eV, which corresponded to CO and COH, respectively[33]. These consequences demonstrated that the obtained S-GQDs were rich in carboxylic groups on the surfaces. The S 2p spectrum of S-GQDs (Fig. 4D) was composed of two peaks at 163.6eV and 168.4eV, respectively, displaying two forms of S structure. The former peak can be fall into two apparent constituents at 163.2eV and 164.8eV, which fitted with the reported 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 positions of the CSC covalent bond of the thiophene-like S, because of the spin-orbit couplings. And the latter peak can be divided into two distinct constituents at 167.6eV and 169.2eV, which agreed with CS (O2)C sulphone bridges[24]. The results revealed the successful S-doping into both the framework and the edge of the GQDs. The possible structure of S-GQDs was given in Scheme 1 . As known, 1,3,6-trinitropyrene possessed the same mother nucleus structure as graphene. It has been proven that 1,3,6-trinitropyrene could occur molecular fusion in hydrothermal reaction to form framwork of graphene[31]. As the nitro groups got powerful electrophilicity, the addition reaction can occur with electron-rich groups. Therefore, −SH groups of 3-mercaptopropionic acid added to pyrene ring in the hydrothermal reaction. The carboxyl groups in MPA linked to GQDs at the same time to exhibit −COOH modification. Also, some S atom might be captured and fused in the graphene lattice. The synergistic effect of S-doping and COOH in GQDs might possess special coordination abilities and corresponding selectivity towards metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective fluorescence response of S-GQDs towards Ag+
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent characteristics in commonly used PBS buffer and the presence of different environmentally-relevant metal ions, inculding Na+, K+, Ag+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Cd2+, Co2+, were studied (Fig. 5 A). When the mixed solution was placed under UV lamp (365nm), S-GQDs solution with Ag+ appeared remarkable fluorescent quenching, however, no notable fluorescence quenching effect was observed in the presence of other metal ions (Fig. 5A). After fluorescent spectrum was tested, the results were further confirmed as shown in Fig. 5B. It is well known that MPA is a commonly used compound with coordination ability towards metal ions because of the existence of carboxyl groups. Using MPA as modifier for CdTe QDs [34], metal nanoculsters [35] and Au nanoparticles [36,37], fluorescence detection for Ag+, Cu2+, Hg2+ was reported. The high selectivity of S-GQDs towards Ag+ may be due to the synergistic effect of S and O functional groups on S-GQDs. In our very recently work, the synthesis of sulfur-doped graphene quantum dots based on water-phase molecular fusion with 1,3,6-trinitropyrene, Na2S, and NaOH in hydrothermal process was achieved. The S-GQDs possessed large OH groups and the S2p spectrum were assigned to CSOxC (x=2, 3, and 4) sulphone bridges. A selective and sensitive response to Pb2+ was revealed compared with GQDs due to the synergistic effects of OH groups and S-doping [38]. That exhibited a different selectivity compared with the S-GQDs in this work. Xu et al. also reported the preparation of S-CQDs with sodium citrate and sodium thiosulfate by a hydrothermal method. The fluorescence of such S-doped C-dots can be effectively and selectively quenched by Fe3+ ions [39]. We viewed that the selectivity of GQDs towards metal ion might have great relationship with its structure and the corresponding characteristics. When the preparation method and the initial materials for the synthesis of GQDs changed, the surface functional groups (e.g. type and distribution) and the doping of heteroatom (e.g. doping concentration and configuration) changed. For instance, the surface COOH might give different interation towards metal ions as compared with surface OH. Also, S shares doping configurations as CSC, CSOxC (x=2, 3, and 4) and CSH when the sulfur source changed. As a result, the physicochemical and electronic properties of GQDs, including charge transport, Fermi level, bandgap, localized electronic state, spin density and so on, can be altered (sometimes drastically). The difference in those characteristics finally determined the selectivity of GQDs.

The pH effect on the fluorescent quenching of S-GQDs caused by Ag+ was further explored. The values of (F0 −F)/F0 have little changes at pH 4.5–7.0 (Fig. 5C). Thus, the neutral solution (pH 7.0) was chosen for further investigation. The kinetic behavior of reactions between the S-GQDs and Ag+ was investigated by plotting relative FL intensities as a function of time. The fast response in 2min might result from the interaction between Ag+ and the surface or edge groups of S-GQDs. Then the reactions reached the quenching equilibrium in less than 7min resulting from the interaction between Ag+ and the internally-doped S of GQDs (Fig. 5D). Therefore, 10min was chosen as reaction equilibrium time in the subsequent detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Ag+ using S-GQDs as fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimized conditions, a quantitative detection of Ag+ was performed. As the concentration of Ag+ increased (Fig. 6 A), a remarkable decrease in FL intensity of the S-GQDs was observed. A good linearity existed in the values of (F0 −F)/F0 as the Ag+ concentration ranged from 0.1 to 130.0μM (Fig. 6B), confirming the validity of the fluorescent detection of Ag+ using S-GQDs as fluorescent probe. The linear regression equations was (F0-F)/F0 =0.0053C-0.005 with R2 =0.9985, where F0 and F are respectively the fluorescence intensities of the S-GQDs solutions in the absence and presence of Ag+ and C is the concentration of Ag+. The detection limit was calculated to be 0.03μM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. Table 1 listed the comparison of various fluorescent probes for Ag+ detection, the detection limit (DL) obtained with the present method was lower than those achieved by organic fluorescent probes [40,41] and carbon QDs [42], but higher than thosed gained by BSA modified CdTe QDs [43] and Nitrogen-doped GQDs [44].

The reliability of the as-prepared S-GQDs in practical application was evaluated by Ag+ detection in environmental sample. Water of local lake was collected and concentrated 50 times (Sample 1). As shown in Table 2 , the detection results of Ag+ ions using S-GQDs and ICP-MS were compared. It was obviously that the result obtained from S-GQDs was closed to that given by standard ICP-MS. The standard addition method was also applied to further evaluate the developped FL sensor. After Ag+ solution was spiked into the matrix of Sample 1, good recoveries were obtained, indicationg the potential application of S-GQDs in real analysis.

A simple and low-cost strategy for one-step synthesis of S-GQDs was developed based on water-phase molecular fusion with 1,3,6-trinitropyrene and 3-mercaptopropionic acid in hydrothermal process. The advantages of the proposed method and the prepared S-GQDs lie in four aspects. (1) Successful doping of S atom in GQDs was proven and S-GQDs exhibited blue emission, excitation-independent photoluminescence behaviors, monolayer-graphene thickness and uniform size of 2.5nm. (2) The S-GQDs showed a selective and sensitive response to Ag+. (3) Based on the fluorescence quenching of S-GQDs by Ag+, a facile and direct fluorescence sensor for sensitive detection of Ag+ was achieved with fast response, wide linear range and low detection limit. Analysis of real sample was also achieved. The ease of the one-step synthesis and the promising performance of the prepared S-GQDs make this work attractive in bioimaging, sensing, optoelectronic devices and catalysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21305127), the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (Y15B050022, Y17B050019), the Science Foundation of Zhejiang Sci-Tech University (13062173-Y) and 521 talent project of ZSTU.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.11.044.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Side reactions in controlling the quality yield, and stability of high quality colloidal nanocrystals

Large, solution-processable graphene quantum dots as light absorbers for photovoltaics
Heteroatom-doped graphene materials: syntheses properties and applications

Waste frying oil as a precursor for one-step synthesis of sulfur-doped carbon dots with pH-sensitive photoluminescence

Single-fluorophore-based fluorescent probes enable dual-channel detection of Ag+ and Hg2+ with high selectivity and sensitivity
One-pot synthesis of carbon nanodots for fluorescence turn-on detection of Ag+ based on the Ag+-induced enhancement of fluorescence
Purification of denatured bovine serum albumin coated CdTe quantum dots for sensitive detection of silver(I) ions
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots: turn-off fluorescent probe for detection of Ag+ ions

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Fengna Xi obtains her PhD from Zhejiang University, China, in 2007. She is currently an associated Professor of Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, China. Her research interests cover analysis and sensors based on functional nanomaterials.
